stem: Define \( d(x,y) \) on the integers by \( d(x,y) = 0 \) if \( x = y \) and \( d(x,y) = 1 \) if \( x \neq y \) (the discrete metric; one can check it satisfies all three metric axioms). Compute \( d([[p]], [[q]]) \).
solution: The discrete metric outputs \( 0 \) only when the two inputs are equal and \( 1 \) whenever they differ; here \( d([[p]],[[q]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the ordinary absolute difference, not the discrete metric's 0/1 output.
This is the signed difference, not the discrete metric's value.
This is the complement of the correct 0/1 value (swaps the roles of equal and unequal).
This is the sum of the two inputs, unrelated to the discrete metric.
This is one more than the correct 0/1 value.
This is two more than the correct 0/1 value.

stem: Define \( d(x,y) = \dfrac{|x-y|}{1+|x-y|} \) for real numbers \( x, y \) (a genuine bounded metric: it is symmetric, vanishes only when \( x=y \), and satisfies the triangle inequality). Compute \( d([[p]], [[q]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This is the raw distance \( |x-y| \), before it is bounded by dividing by \( 1+|x-y| \).
This inverts the fraction instead of putting \( |x-y| \) in the numerator.
This replaces the denominator's \( 1 \) with \( |x+y| \), which is not the formula.
This drops the absolute value, so a sign error can creep into the numerator and denominator.
This adds \( 1 \) to the correct value.
This is one minus the correct value, the wrong complement of the formula.

stem: For a fixed positive constant \( c = [[c]] \), define \( d(x,y) = [[c]]\,|x-y| \) on the real numbers (scaling the usual absolute-value metric by any positive constant always produces another metric). Compute \( d([[p]], [[q]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to scale by the constant \( c \).
This adds \( c \) instead of multiplying by it.
This drops the absolute value, so the sign of \( x-y \) can flip the result.
This uses the sum \( x+y \) instead of the difference \( x-y \).
This is off from the correct value by \( c \).
This is off from the correct value by \( c \) in the other direction.

stem: For points \( P = ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \) and \( Q = ([[x2]], [[y2]]) \) in the plane, define \( d(P,Q) = \max(|x_1-x_2|,\ |y_1-y_2|) \) (the Chebyshev, or sup, metric; one can check the metric axioms hold). Compute \( d(P,Q) \).
solution: The two coordinate gaps are \( |x_1-x_2| \) and \( |y_1-y_2| \); the Chebyshev distance is their maximum, \( d(P,Q) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the taxicab (Manhattan) distance, the sum of the coordinate gaps, not their maximum.
This uses the minimum of the coordinate gaps instead of the maximum.
This multiplies the coordinate gaps instead of taking their maximum.
This mismatches the coordinates, comparing \(x_1\) to \(y_1\) instead of to \(x_2\).
This is one more than the correct distance.
This is one less than the correct distance.
